channel.title: Responsible AI & Neurodivergence, Naba Rizvi
channel.description: Daily curated feed on responsible AI for neurodivergent people and serious mental illness.

item.title: Lost in Delusion: Examining LLM Safety Under User Delusions and Distress, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Matched multi-turn simulations across six LLMs find models detect distress at similar rates regardless of framing yet suppress safety interventions up to 4.5x once distress is embedded in delusion. Only delusion-aware prompting closes the gap, so the authors argue delusional framing should be treated as a distinct risk signal that overrides conversational accommodation.

item.title: MHSafeEval: Role-Aware Interaction-Level Evaluation of Mental Health Safety in Large Language Models, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Introduces a role-aware safety taxonomy (perpetrator, instigator, facilitator, enabler) and an agent-based framework that probes harm through adversarial multi-turn interactions, revealing cumulative safety failures that static, single-response benchmarks systematically miss.

item.title: AI Safety Training Can be Clinically Harmful, arXiv, 2026
item.description: Testing four models on prolonged exposure and CBT restructuring scenarios, therapeutic appropriateness collapsed at high severity even as surface acknowledgment stayed near perfect. The authors find RLHF safety alignment disrupts the therapeutic mechanism of grounding patients, a paradox where harm-avoidance measures undermine clinical effectiveness.

item.title: "Are we writing an advice column for Spock here?" Understanding Stereotypes in AI Advice for Autistic Users, CHI 2026
item.description: An audit of 345,000 responses from six LLMs plus interviews with autistic participants finds that disclosing autism pushed models to recommend avoiding social events, confrontation, new experiences, and relationships. Participants split on whether this felt affirming or infantilizing, surfacing a safety-opportunity paradox in personalized advice.

item.title: A cautionary tale for AI and machine learning in psychiatry, Translational Psychiatry, 2026
item.description: This perspective warns that gains in prediction accuracy often fail to translate into clinical benefit, flagging weaknesses in rigor, reliability, and interpretability. It proposes a human-assisted AI framework with incremental feedback and dynamic collaboration to address bias and build clinician trust.

item.title: Chatbots Need Guardrails to Prevent Delusions and Psychosis, IEEE Spectrum, 2026
item.description: Surveys a fast-moving regulatory landscape: the EU AI Act's August 2026 disclosure and adversarial-testing duties, California and New York break and self-harm rules, Washington's ban on manipulative design, and China's draft limits on emotional manipulation, arguing makers must encode clinical and ethical considerations into chatbots.
